topics.title: Port Security and Iraq Conflict
topics.summary: The show critiques the decision to allow foreign entities to manage domestic ports and criticizes the current state of the war in Iraq.

topics.title: Port Security and Radio Industry Discussion
topics.summary: The studio explores the complexities of port security measures, the impact of government regulations on radio broadcasting, and internal production dynamics regarding show segments and talent performance.

topics.title: Radio Show Production and Media Commentary
topics.summary: The studio discusses internal production feedback, upcoming segments like "Better Half," the decline of Olympic viewership due to time delays, and the evolution of reality television programs.

topics.title: NBC Jingle Packages and Eric the Midget
topics.summary: The studio explores old NBC jingle packages that forced specific phrasing, followed by a discussion regarding the presence and personality of guest Eric the Midget.

topics.title: Eric the Midget's American Idol Knowledge
topics.summary: The studio features a segment where Eric the Midget demonstrates his extensive knowledge of American Idol contestants and trivia while discussing his personal life and upcoming radio show.

topics.title: Artie Lange and Vending Machine Habits
topics.summary: The studio discusses Artie Lange’s heavy consumption of sugary snacks from the office vending machine and his recent performance at a comedy roast.

topics.title: Vending Machine Snacks and Show Feedback
topics.summary: The studio explores the contents of a vending machine while discussing internal feedback regarding show performance and interpersonal dynamics.

topics.title: Radio Show Feedback and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses listener feedback regarding the show's promotion of satellite radio services, a prank call involving Ronnie Mund, and recent reports concerning Jenny McCarthy.

topics.title: Artie's Relationship Struggles
topics.summary: The studio discusses Artie's tumultuous relationship with Dana, focusing on his personal struggles with weight, substance use, and the ultimatum she has presented regarding his need for therapy.

topics.title: Relationship Dynamics and Personal Struggles
topics.summary: The studio explores a guest's personal struggles with depression, his marriage, and the complexities of balancing professional life with personal relationships.

topics.title: Celebrity Gossip and Fact-Checking Segment
topics.summary: The studio features a segment where guests evaluate four celebrity news stories to determine which one is fake.

topics.title: Port Security and Celebrity Weight Loss
topics.summary: The studio discusses the security risks of foreign-owned ports, celebrity weight fluctuations following surgery, and a caller recounts childhood abuse by his father.

topics.title: Celebrity Relationships and Personal Anecdotes
topics.summary: The studio explores various topics including personal stories about family, celebrity dating rumors involving John Stamos and Natalie Portman, a study on the physiological differences between masturbation and intercourse, and discussions regarding magazine readership.

topics.title: Current Events and Celebrity Gossip
topics.summary: The studio covers a variety of topics including celebrity rumors, reports on anti-war protesters at military funerals, concerns regarding Dubai's involvement in U.S. ports, and the passage of a Pennsylvania law protecting the names of legacy musical groups.

transcript.text: Yeah, good morning everybody. Hey now. Well, we've got a wide open show today and that's a good thing because we have lots to get to.
Now, everybody comes up to me and they say, Howard, really, what's going on with this thing with the ports? You know, like...
People are coming up to you nasty.
Yeah, what should we think? You know, some people say it's no big deal if the government allows ports to be managed and controlled by the nation of Dubai.
In other words, they say the Coast Guard is really the ones who are in charge of inspections and so isn't this much ado about nothing?
And then I have to sit them down and I explain to them, of course not. Very, very important issue. It is true the Coast Guard is in charge of managing our port security.
And by the way, only 6%, this is the figure that they released, 6% of containers that come in through our ports are really examined by security or the Coast Guard.

And not even the Coast Guard. Let me tell you how it works. Coast Guard is in charge of security but the companies who run these ports are in charge of executing the rules that the Coast Guard lays down for them.

See? So, of course this is a big deal. If you're going to put Dubai in charge of 6 ports where canisters, containers come into this country, which is the main way to smuggle nuclear weapons into this country...
And some of the largest and most active ports on the East Coast, mind you.

That's right. East Coast most populated, you understand what Robin is saying. If you're going to allow Dubai a country that sometimes helps us and sometimes doesn't, two of the guys from about 9/11 were from Dubai.
This is a government that has done business with the Taliban. You've got to understand something. The Coast Guard lays down the rules. You've got to comply with them and put them into effect.

How would you, in your right mind, ever, in a country that has been threatened by terrorists, put countries who do business with terrorists in charge of executing the rules and the laws of this country?
You've got to be insane. Not only that, you're putting money in the pocket of people who might be financing terrorism.
Listen, we're doing a horrible job. Forget about Dubai. We're doing a horrible job of guarding our ports.
And quite frankly, all this money that went into this horrible wasted effort in Iraq could have been put into tremendous security measures in our ports.

Do you realize how much safer we'd be? See, this is an issue of safety for people. When they go to bed at night, they don't want to think about Dubai somehow being involved and cooperating with the Coast Guard in terms of examining canisters and being on the lookout.
Why? That's like Sirius putting XM in charge. I mean, you've got to be nuts.
When I was a longshoreman, I don't know, maybe it's different now, but I was a longshoreman in the early '90s. I had full access to orange juice that went out to the world.
That's right. That's no joke.
And these ships would come in from Brazil with orange juice that went all over the country.
And who was watching you?
They had these government guys there who were supposed to be, you know, inspectors and stuff, but most of them just played B-Knock.
Well, you've hit it on the head, New York Times. Very few cargo inspections are conducted.
This is from an article by Clark Kent Ervin.
Clark Kent Ervin?
That's right. Who was the Inspector General of the Homeland Security Department. I know what you're thinking, Clark Kent.
But, hey, didn't Nicholas Cage just name his kid Kalel?
Yes, he did.
So what's wrong with Clark Kent?
Somebody should have named a child Clark Kent.
The point is, there's a lot of nuts in the world.
Clark Kent Ervin was the Inspector General of Homeland Security Department from 2003 to 2004.
And this guy says that the Coast Guard merely sets the standards that ports are to follow and review.
They're not there doing the container searches themselves.
That's right. You've got to meet the standards that the Coast Guard sets. That's the job of the port operator.
And that's what the President is doing, doing a bait and switch.
Oh, no, the Coast Guard is still being charged there.
Port operators are responsible for hiring security officers guarding the cargo and overseeing its unloading.
So, I mean, there it is from a guy who is in charge of Homeland Security.
Let me repeat that real quick here.
Meeting the standards each day is the job of port operators.
They're responsible for hiring security officers guarding the cargo and overseeing its unloading.
So, come on, let's stop the nonsense.
I can't sleep at night thinking that Dubai is managing our ports.
And neither can you.
I'm sick over the fact that we're wasting all this money in Iraq.
I opened up the paper today, and there it is.
Iraq erupts near the brink of civil war.
Yet we were supposed to be able to prevent a civil war by being there?
Right.
Doesn't seem even that possible.
No, we created one. There were three factions in Iraq for anybody who hasn't been paying attention, who want to kill each other.
The only guy who contained all three factions and put them in their place was Saddam Hussein.
There was a perfect balance over there.
What we've done now is opened ourselves up to more misery.
More than 114 people, including some clerics, have been killed since Wednesday when bombs destroyed the Golden Mosque.
A Shiite shrine north of Baghdad unleashing a wave of reprisal attacks against Sunni Arabs.
They're talking about that this could cause a massive civil war, and our exit strategy faces collapse.
It's very funny because they're arguing about whether it's a civil war already.
According to some experts, this has been going on for some time.
It hasn't just started. This isn't an eruption.
This is just the full-blown evidence that these guys have been fighting ever since we've been there.
Yeah.
The three siblings, now that Daddy's dead, are fighting over the control of the family estate.
And that's exactly what we're in the middle of.
And it is a civil war already.
I don't mean to be serious on the first thing in the morning, but I do want to explain to people, because people do ask me about it.
And you've got to understand what's going on there somewhat.
Our president has put us in a pickle.
And just think of the billions and billions of dollars if it was handed over to securing our ports.
We would be so much safer now, and Saddam Hussein would be in power.
Big effing deal. The guy was nothing.
The only thing he was good at was keeping maniacs from killing each other and keeping Iran out of Iraq
and keeping every other Arab country out of Iraq.
So where are we at? What the lesson is for today is you've got to really think long and hard about who your president's going to be next time around.
I tried to get somewhat political with the show while we were on testicle radio, but people would write me letters and tell me to shut up.
I don't know what I'm talking about.
They like their coma that they're in.
Well, you know, everybody won't go down together doing dick jokes.
Look at this. I'm reading, you know, they've got all that stuff going on in Iraq.
And so whose fault is it? Iran's president is blaming Zionists and foreign forces for Iraq.
Well, it's smart. Let me tell you one more thing.
Because of the bombing of the Shiite temple.
You bring up a good point. Whenever there is misery in the Middle East, and you've got to understand something,
the people running the governments, these Arab governments, you know, the people, Palestinians themselves, the people are miserable.
The Egyptians, they can't get anything from their government.
There are more deaths than Egypt.
The government can't help people when they're ill, when they're sick.
It's a shambles.
And what these governments do, the second they run into trouble, they blame the Jews and they blame the United States.
And it's smart, because the people over there have been so used to this, they're brainwashed.
And right away they go, oh my God, we've got to get the Jews in the United States.
And they get their mind off the fact that their government can't supply water to them, or health care, or anything else.
It is the most brilliant move a government can do. Hitler did it.
Things are wrong in Germany. We've got to band together and get rid of the Jews, and anyone who loves freedom.
And this is going on and on and on throughout history.
We are in a quagmire in Iraq. Don't kid yourself, don't think for a minute that this is a good policy.
Getting in there was wrong. It's created a mess.
Afghanistan good. Clean up the mess. Iraq. No wind situation. Quagmire.
Creating an Afghanistan.
If Saddam Hussein was really a threat, we should have dropped a nuclear bomb, not cost one American life.
That's it. You really want to commit? You want to get rid of a country that's filled with terrorists?
Wipe them all out. Start over again. Flatten them out.
Don't risk our lives, don't risk our security, and don't waste their time.
If Iran, if North Korea's got the goddamn bombs and they're making them, get rid of them now.
Blow up the whole fucking country.
Well, if they've got the bomb, we're in trouble. It's too late already.
You want to do what you're afraid of, sir? Do what Truman did to Japan. Here, stop your nonsense.
But you do it before they get the ultimate weapon.
That's right. Do it now. Iran's a problem. Get rid of them. Wipe them out. Put your money where your mouth is.
Well, the problem with that now is that if we did that to Iran, Korea would say, "Well, we must be next,"
and they would launch their own bomb.
Take them out both. Same time. China. Everyone.
We're going to take out China, too.
Take them out. Why not? While we're at it.
You know, my question is, for all these religious people, could they answer me why God put all the oil in all the wrong places?
I know. It's horrible. We had oil. We had a lot of oil. We still have oil in this country.
Yeah, but the rest of it is in places that it's just horrible to go to.
That's why when I went to my -- I was visiting my daughter in college, and I met a young lady who's a friend of ours
who is busy converting cow manure into methane. I was so happy that somebody was doing something.
Yeah, they're busy in those dormitories. Let me tell you, I like it, but what can I tell you?
We've got a president who's anti-science. He doesn't want even stem cell research.
I'm not sure he wants cow manure to turn into methane. I don't know.
No, actually, he has changed his position just a little bit.
He is saying that now we need alternative fuels.
Hybrid cars. Way to wake up.
And why did God put, like, those Hawaiian beaches so far from New York Hill?
I know. It's just not -- God is not a nice guy.
He should be like where Belmar is, Hawaii.
I know. I hate that California can just do a hop, skip, and a jump right over to the Four Seasons in Hawaii.
Yeah. And so, New York is where civilization is. It's going to be closer.
I'm a Schwarzenegger that doesn't need another vacation running around on the phone.
We need it, right here in New York. Come on, Tom. You're on the air in Georgia.
Hey, good morning. What's happening?
Hey, is it Howard?
Yes.
Well, where did you call?
Tom Hye.
Hi, Tom. Actually, I'm from Detroit here.
Tom, what can I do for you?
Yeah. Hey, I'm a trucker out here.
I just had a couple examples of our security and how well it's in place.
But last year, the day before the Super Bowl --
Don't tell me you're getting to the orange juice like I already did.
No, no. I actually had a delivery that went into Jacksonville the day before the Super Bowl.
And I'm just thanking myself.
I was probably 500 yards from the stadium where they played the game the day before Super Bowl.
And you'd think security would probably be a little bit tighter than what it was.
But I mean, I'm thanking myself as a trucker in what somebody would be capable of doing.
Exactly. Well, you're scaring me, and that's a good thing because --
And another example was I actually got into a military base and got past the guard shack by simply telling --
Well, I did make a wrong turn and ended up getting into a military base.
But when I got to the guard shack, they simply asked me what I was doing.
And I said, "I made a wrong turn. I just need to go in here and turn around."
And I mean, if somebody with the wrong reasons, you know, that simple, you know, can get into a place like that and do what --
Yeah. Our security is very lax, and let's agree that --
We've been very lucky that nobody's trying to do anything.
So if we just took all that money that we dumped into that hellhole of a rack, we would have just tremendous security.
Oh, New Orleans would have been rebuilt already.
Joe, you're on the air.
Howard, hey now.
Hey now, Joe.
Hey, don't buddy. Listen, first time caller, long time listener.
All right, Joe.
I got my Sirius last year for Christmas.
Nice. Good. You did some advanced planning, and I'm glad because I understand that it's still hard to get Sirius in a lot of the major stores.
In fact, we saw a report yesterday that Sirius is outselling XM2 to 1, 64 percent versus 34 percent, and that makes me pleased.
Oh, yeah. I love it. I love it. I love you guys. I love Robin, Artie.
I just wanted to tell you, I agree with your views on the support thing. I think it's ridiculous.
I'm a carpenter, and I work in the refineries every now and then.
I'll tell you what. The security in there is real lax.
Yeah. Our own government admits it. Six percent of containers are investigated. I mean, come on.
What are you talking about? That's nothing. Darren, go ahead.
Hey, what's happening?
Hey, brother.
Dude, first off, Artie Lange should be the president.
Of course.
And he should do it from Vegas. Let's have him do it from Vegas.
He built the White House in Vegas.
You're saying we're whiskey and coke.
He couldn't do any worse a job than being done right now, I'll be honest with him.
He's got the same heart now.
How can we give this to these people? How dare we even think about such a thing?
Right, of course.
Do we have these people? No, that's my point.
Well, the committee that actually okayed the sale is basically a rubber stamp committee.
They don't really do investigations.
That's right.
Can they read? Do you know how to read books?
The president blinked yesterday, and he said --
Well, I guess --
The president blinked yesterday, and he said, "Listen, we're going to give a couple days here to investigate all this."
Yeah, everybody's wanting to take a little more time.
Yeah, the president typically does that.
He gives breathing time, and then everyone forgets about it, and then everything just goes down as normal.
There was this one truck driver, just a quick story by these truckers about what they could do and stuff.
Right.
You know, the orange juice was transported in those tankers.
It looked like oil tankers, you know?
Right.
And there was this one guy who would come in a redneck from Texas, who was a trucker,
who would come in once a month, and he always had cash on him.
And sometimes he'd borrow money from, like, this mini loan shark that was hanging around.
And he would always come back with more money to give him.
And finally, we said, "Where do you get all this money from when you leave?"
Once a month, this guy would take his tanker to Mexico, put 100 Mexicans in the tanker,
and lock it up, and go across the border like he had orange juice,
and take it into San Antonio, and would get paid, like, 100 bucks for each head.
And he would always pay the loan shark, like, 40% vig.
Like, you know, he always had extra money.
The guy finally got caught.
But he was doing that for, like, a year.
He got arrested with the -- it was a government raid.
The guy -- these guys came in and just took the guy away and changed.
Wow.
But that's what some of these truckers are up to.
Like, you have no idea what's going on.
Let's go to Kurt.
There's a lot of people who want to get through.
Kurt's in Virginia.
Go ahead, Kurt.
Hey, Howard, man.
You hit the nail right on the head about Saddam.
I was a former soldier.
I was in Iraq for 15 months.
The whole entire time, that's what we all said.
We just got to leave this guy in control.
He's the only one that can keep all these people, you know, from killing each other.
They got to go right to that prison where they keep them.
Give them a nice shave.
Give them a military outfit.
Send them back into the field and pray to God that he can tame those monsters again.
See, I think they've thrown it.
Because once he was found crawling around in a hole in the ground,
the people realized he wasn't that powerful.
It's going to be a very tough -- it's like the Wizard of Oz when they pulled back the --
it's going to be tough to rebuild him, and thus his two torturing sons.
I mean, you know, the guy would have a huge problem on his hands,
but I still say he's the guy for the job.
He is.
He's the guy for the job, and you nailed it right on the head.
I mean, nobody else does that.
You're the only one with the balls to say that, and you got it 100 percent right.
Oh, I've taken my hits for saying it.
People think it's horrible, but --
Well, actually, yesterday I saw a news commentator, I think, on one of the stations --
I can't remember what I was looking at -- who essentially said the same thing.
We had the problem of a tyrant when we had Saddam Hussein in power,
and now we have a problem of a country we cannot control.
That's right, and let me tell you something.
Saddam Hussein was more afraid of terrorists than we are.
He lived in fear that there would be an uprising,
and his country would be taken away from him.
He was more of an ally than you'll ever know, and guess what?
He had no weapons of mass destruction, and that's the truth,
and this guy was not our number one problem.
Now we got a problem.
Now we got a big problem.
Jason, you're on the air, and thank you for that call.
Hey, how are you doing today?
Hey, Jason.
I just wanted to say, you know, I see all these things going on in government today,
and, you know, at 25 years old, I have to ask,
how many more freedoms am I going to lose by the time I get to 50?
Oh, all of them probably.
Well, you know, a big -- when elections start -- when is the elections off of --
The 2006 elections?
Yeah, they're coming up.
Yeah, November of this year.
November of this year.
And once again, our government, you know, has wisely brought to the forefront the FCC
to talk about how they're going to really crack down now on radio.
Yeah.
Which is good, because it's like what the Arabs do with their governments
when they blame the Jews.
Our country blames the media for all the problems, and it's good.
It keeps all the yahoos and the dummies in this country busy,
all the religious nuts who are against abortions and all this other stuff.
Keeps them busy.
They feel like things are going on.
Yeah, Bush is a good guy.
He's going to find CBS $550,000 for that breast that we saw.
My children are safe.
My kids are safe.
Let's go to sleep.
God bless George W. Bush in the United States of America.
I mean, this is insanity what's going on in this country,
and I don't even know that anyone can ever get this country back under control again.
It's so far down the tubes, it's not even funny.
Now, let me come on.
I mean, does anybody read the paper?
Well, of course you do.
Yeah, but they're reading the funny papers.
We're reading the paper, and our audience is reading the paper.
The problem is the yahoos are taking over.
I mean, we've got religious kooks in this country who are a bunch of dummies.
They're going downhill fast.
They want to go to Jesus.
They're going to go to an imaginary guy in the sky,
and he's going to make things better for them than for the Jews,
than for the Arabs, and from everyone else.
We got people who are buying into a fairy tale.
I mean, come on.
What are we doing?
Yeah, it's like a bunch of children you're talking about here.
Flavio says I'm wrong.
Go ahead, Flavio, because I hope I am wrong.
Yeah, Howard, how are you doing, Sammy?
Yeah, sure, go ahead.
Yeah, I'm a teamster over here.
I'm important to work almost every day, man.
As far as the security goes, I mean, first of all,
100 percent of the containers on the way out of the port
go through a radiation detector.
Now, of course, the problem is now the container is already in the U.S.
before it goes through the radiation detector.
So the security problem is really overseas where these things are loaded.
I don't know what they're doing over there
to make sure the container never gets on the ship.
Well, Flavio, while things where you are might be okay,
the horrible statistic is that 6 percent of the containers
here in the United States are examined.
But Howard, what you missed in is,
see how it works is you go get checked in, right?
You drive the container through a radiation detector on the way out.
If that sets off an alarm for radioactive material inside the box,
then they bring the container over to the side
and they do a thorough examination.
But 100 percent of them go through a radiation detector.
And that's at every single terminal here.
Any ship could detonate or launch even before all the way into a port.
Well, that's a problem.
Of course.
And secondly, and secondly, and secondly,
you know there are ways of beating the system.
You know, we've got airport security.
There's not a new special that doesn't go by
where a young reporter wants to make his bones,
goes over to U.S. airports, sneaks about 20 pistols through,
and then comes out and says, "Look at all the guns I got through,"
and embarrasses the airport security.
You're talking about our ports are very lax.
Yeah, and you're worried about what's in a container
and what they might be bringing into the country to harm us.
But what about the people?
Well, I mean, don't get me wrong, Howard.
I'm not a fan of letting these guys take over our ports,
but it's not like we're running it right now.
I mean, it's being run by the British right now.
Well, you're absolutely right.
You know, they're our allies, but I don't like any of these companies
taking over anything here.
You're absolutely right.
Why is somebody outside of the country at all?
Let me tell you what it is.
Owning certain things.
That's exactly right.
Both of you are right, because understand that the Coast Guard,
the United States government, is in charge of security.
The people who have to act out that security,
act upon that security, and make it flow and make it work
are these companies who are foreign owned.
Now, you might say that the British are true allies.
You can't say that the city of Dubai is on our team.
You can't.
No, you can't say that.
No, that's for sure.
But, I mean, I'm just wanting to let you know, Howard,
that it's not, you know, 6%.
That's the...
I believe that 6% are open and checked, but...
That's the opening and checking.
That's the opening and checking.
If you had the budget that we have over in Iraq,
every one of those canisters would have to be opened and checked.
And let me tell you something.
And our borders would be sealed.
Because, you know, while you're talking about the ports,
we still have huge borders with Canada and Mexico
where we have vast problems.
Right.
All right.
Howard, the other thing, you can't really do that, though, man.
I mean, if you saw...
I mean, I was at the pier the other day for five and a half hours
trying to get a container out of there.
That's how many guys are in there trying to pick this stuff up.
Can you imagine if they had to open everything?
Yeah, I can.
I mean, you know what?
It would be a tremendous inconvenience.
And the problem is, we don't want to be inconvenienced.
But then when there's a 9/11 or a nuclear weapon,
I'd be detonated.
Well, you know what?
Maybe it's time some sacrifices have to be made.
And we're going to have to wait for goods.
And maybe things aren't going to be so perfect.
But you know what?
We won't get our asses blown up by a nuclear weapon.
And, you know, again, I know what you're saying.
We're in a society.
I'm guilty of it, too.
I want things fast.
You know, I stand by my microwave oven
and I go, "This is son of a bitch coffee.
Can't they figure out a way to heat it in 10 seconds instead of 30?"
I mean, I want things done quickly, too.
But listen, what are we talking about here?
We're talking about our lives.
We're talking about our families.
I agree with you on that.
We're talking about Howard 100 and Howard 101.
I mean, we've got to be safe.
I'm talking about the homeland.
I missed the old days when the mob ran the port.
They knew how to do it.
Come on.
Well, maybe.
At least they're American.
Yeah, they know what they're doing.
Come on.
And they have something vested in this country.
Yes, Neal.
Yeah, for a second.
I almost thought I'd turn on Air America.
I know, I know.
I got carried away this morning.
Once in a while, I get a little political
because I do get worked up about this stuff.
No, I understand.
I just want to stay really quick.
I want to thank you for coming to Syria.
Your evolution has happened so quickly
and how comfortable you sound.
Your radio stations are amazing.
I was looking forward for it for so long.
You've definitely fallen into your group pretty quick.
Hey, Neal.
I got to tell you, you remind me of a point I wanted to make.
You realize that our show and these channels
have really officially been on the air
for only six or seven weeks?
Amazing.
It is amazing.
And I lose sight of that.
I was in a meeting with Tim and Bubba,
and I'm doing my usual ranting and raving
about where we should be at.
And I'm talking about impatient.
And Tim said to me,
"You realize you've been on the air for six weeks?"
Yep.
And I said, "You are so right.
I got to take a breath and think about it.
We've got a full-service news department
that services our fans about all things that go on in the channels.
We've got a morning show that's up and running."
I was listening last night to the "Superfan" roundtable
with Mutt and "King of All Blacks."
In fact, they called in.
I don't know if you heard it.
I called in during the show
because I had to set those guys straight a little bit.
What were they carrying on about this time?
They were talking about the Ronnie roast
and whether or not Ronnie was interrupting too much.
And I called in and I said,
"Look, on most roasts, you watch them on TV.
You can see the guy being roasted
and you can see him squirming or being uncomfortable.
You can gauge his reaction to the jokes.
We're on radio.
I got to allow for Ronnie or Ralph to interrupt once in a while
so that you can hear that these guys are either upset or calm
or whatever is going on."
And it does interrupt the flow of the comedian.
They're right.
I said, "But, you know, as a guy who runs the show,
I've got to at least give you some indication
that the guy is still in the room and he's reacting."
Yeah, well, Yucco had a...
You know, he felt that his rhythm had been through.
I would before you hang up on me.
Yeah, and Yucco's...
It's true, Yucco's...
